chunks.text: Stereo. HC JD A 38. Judgment Sheet IN THE LAHORE HIGH COURT, BAHAWALPUR BENCH, BAHAWALPUR. JUDICIAL DEPARTMENT Murder Reference No.07 of 2018 (The State Vs. Muhammad Imran) Criminal Appeal No.176-J of 2018 (Muhammad Imran Vs. The State and another) Date of hearing: 05.10.2021. Appellant by: Sardar Israr Hussain Dahir, Advocate (learned defence counsel appointed on State expense). State by: Complainant by: Ch. Asghar Ali Gill, Deputy Prosecutor General. Mr. Muhammad Jamil Akhtar Chohan, Advocate. SADIQ MAHMUD KHURRAM, J. – Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh (convict) was tried by the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Rahim Yar Khan in case FIR No. 579 of 2013 dated 14.10.2013 registered at Police Station Rukan Pur, District Rahim Yar Khan in respect of offences under sections 302 and 324 PPC for committing the Qatl-i-Amd of Haseena Bibi wife of Haji Ahmad (deceased) and Fatima Bibi daughter of Zafar Hussain (deceased). The learned trial court vide judgment dated 13.03.2018, convicted Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh (convict) and sentenced him as infra: Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh:- i) Death on two counts under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Haseena Bibi wife of Haji Ahmad (deceased) and Fatima Bibi daughter of M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 2 Zafar Hussain (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.200,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of each of the two deceased, in case of default thereof, the convict was further directed to undergo six months of simple imprisonment for each default. The convict was ordered to be hanged by his neck till dead. ii) Rigorous Imprisonment for ten years under section 324 PPC for attempting to commit Qatl-i-Amd of Azra Bibi (PW-4) and directed to pay fine of Rs.50,000/- and in default of payment of fine the convict was directed to undergo further Simple Imprisonment for three months. iii) Rigorous Imprisonment for ten years under section 324 PPC for attempting to commit Qatl-i-Amd of Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and directed to pay fine of Rs.50,000/- and in default of payment of fine the convict was directed to undergo further Simple Imprisonment for three months.

Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 2 Zafar Hussain (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.200,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of each of the two deceased, in case of default thereof, the convict was further directed to undergo six months of simple imprisonment for each default. The convict was ordered to be hanged by his neck till dead. ii) Rigorous Imprisonment for ten years under section 324 PPC for attempting to commit Qatl-i-Amd of Azra Bibi (PW-4) and directed to pay fine of Rs.50,000/- and in default of payment of fine the convict was directed to undergo further Simple Imprisonment for three months. iii) Rigorous Imprisonment for ten years under section 324 PPC for attempting to commit Qatl-i-Amd of Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and directed to pay fine of Rs.50,000/- and in default of payment of fine the convict was directed to undergo further Simple Imprisonment for three months. The learned trial court also directed that the sentences awarded to the convict namely Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh were to run concurrently. The convict namely Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh was also extended the benefit available under Section 382-B of Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 by the learned trial court. 2. Feeling aggrieved, Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh (convict) lodged Criminal Appeal No.176-J of 2018 through jail assailing his conviction and sentences. The learned trial court submitted Murder Reference No.07 of 2018 under section 374 Cr.P.C. seeking confirmation or otherwise of the sentences of death awarded to the appellant namely Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh. We intend to dispose of the Criminal Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 and Murder Reference No.07 of 2018 through this single judgment. 3. Precisely, the necessary facts of the prosecution case, as narrated by Jam Haji (PW-1), the complainant of the FIR, are as under:- M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 3 “Stated that 03 years and 03 months ago at 3.00 p.m, I alongwith Ghulam Nazik and Ghulam Raziq PWS were standing outside my house near the gate on the Southern side; that there is a small gate of my Hawali towards Eastern side; that my wife Mst. Haseena Bibi was sitting on a cot inside the Hawali and was taking meal while my daughter- in-law namely Zakia Bibi was also present inside the house; that accused present in Court, who is my real nephew, entered in the Hawali through the small gate and started to cut tree standing near Southern side of the house, upon which my wife forbade him that these were sunny days and he should not cut the tree.

Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 3 “Stated that 03 years and 03 months ago at 3.00 p.m, I alongwith Ghulam Nazik and Ghulam Raziq PWS were standing outside my house near the gate on the Southern side; that there is a small gate of my Hawali towards Eastern side; that my wife Mst. Haseena Bibi was sitting on a cot inside the Hawali and was taking meal while my daughter- in-law namely Zakia Bibi was also present inside the house; that accused present in Court, who is my real nephew, entered in the Hawali through the small gate and started to cut tree standing near Southern side of the house, upon which my wife forbade him that these were sunny days and he should not cut the tree. At this, accused became furious and told that he will cut them; that on hearing the noise, I alongwith above said PWS entered inside the gate and within our view, accused inflicted hatchet blow on the neck of my wife. As soon as Mst.Zakia Bibi stepped forward, he also gave hatchet blow to her which hit on her left ear and she fell down on the ground and he gave repeated blows of hatchet which hit on her back. On seeing us, accused went outside the house from small gate and rushed towards Northern side and at a distance of two acres there was a pacca water course where my daughter Mst.Azra Bibi alongwith her minor daughter namely Fatima aged 02 years was washing the clothes. Accused present in the Court gave repeated blows of hatchet to my daughter Azra Bibi at the water course and pressed throat of minor Fatima Bibi and threw her in the water course. Due to the injuries, my wife Haseena Bibi and grand daughter Fatima Bibi died while Mst.Zakia Bibi my daughter-in law and my daughter Azra Bibi were seriously injured. The people of the locality informed the police and police came at the spot and I got recorded my statement Exh.PA to Baggu Ahmad SI of P.S.Rukken Pur which bears my signature in Urdu language which is Exh.PA/1. ” 4. After the formal investigation of the case, the report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court, wherein the appellant namely Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh was sent to face trial. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 21.04.2014, to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5.

After the formal investigation of the case, the report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court, wherein the appellant namely Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh was sent to face trial. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 21.04.2014, to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5. The prosecution in order to prove its case, got statements of as many as nineteen witnesses recorded. The ocular account of the case was furnished by Jam Haji (PW-1), Ghulam Raziq (PW-2), Zakia Bibi M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 4 (PW-3) and Azra Bibi (PW-4). Yasir Mehmood 1600/HC (PW-8) stated that on 14.10.2013, Baggu Ahmad, SI (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, handed over to him one sealed parcel said to contain blood stained earth and subsequently also handed over to him a sealed parcel said to contain the recovered hatchet and on 11.12.2013 he handed over both the said parcels to Niaz Ahmad 349/C (PW-6) for their onward transmission to the office of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore. Hasnain Ahmad 1683/C (PW-9) stated that on 14.10.2013 he escorted the dead bodies of the deceased namely Haseena Bibi and Fatima Bibi to the hospital for their post mortem examinations and also escorted Azra Bibi (PW- 4) and Zakia Bibi (PW-3) to the hospital for their medical examinations and after the post mortem examinations had been conducted he received the last worn clothes of the deceased. Ghulam Hussain 1227/C (PW-12) stated that on 02.11.2013 he was handed over the warrants of arrest of the appellant, however, the same could not be executed and he made his report (Exh.PS) and further stated that on 21.11.2013 he was handed over the proclamation under section 87 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 for its publication and his report on the same was (Exh. PT). Khuda Bkahsh (PW-13) stated that he identified the dead bodies of the deceased and after the post mortem examinations, the Medical Officer handed over the last worn clothes of the deceased to the police.

PT). Khuda Bkahsh (PW-13) stated that he identified the dead bodies of the deceased and after the post mortem examinations, the Medical Officer handed over the last worn clothes of the deceased to the police. Riaz Ahmad Patwari, (PW-18) prepared the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PX). Baggu Ahmad, SI (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, investigated the case from 14.10.2013 till 29.10.2013, and M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 5 detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. Muhammad Ijaz, SI (PW-14) stated that on 14.10.2013 he recorded the formal F.I.R (Exh.PU) and also investigated the case from 30.10.2013 till 04.01.2014, arrested the appellant on 10.12.2013 and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. 6. The prosecution also got Dr. Sumera Sarwar (PW-15) examined, who on 14.10.2013 was posted as demonstrator at the Sheikh Zayed Hospital, Rahim Yar Khan and received the dead bodies of the deceased namely Haseena Bibi and Fatima Bibi. On 15.10.2013, Dr. Sumera Sarwar (PW-15) conducted the postmortem examination of the dead body of Haseena Bibi wife of Haji Ahmad (deceased). Dr. Sumera Sarwar (PW-15), on examining the dead body of the deceased namely Haseena Bibi wife of Haji Ahmad, observed as under:- “DETAILS OF INJURIES. 1). An incised wound measuring 8 c x 2 cm from lateral to the lower border of right sternocleidomastoid extending to the lower end of the occiput. Depth of wound was 3 cm cutting all vessels, muscles and exposing cervical vertebrae. Middle part of the wound was 8 cm below right ear lobule...................................... OPINION. After conducting thorough autopsy, I was of the opinion that injury No.1 caused by sharp edge weapon which damaged the major blood vessels and tissues of neck leading to massive hemorrhages and shock was sufficient to cause death in the normal course of life.”

OPINION. After conducting thorough autopsy, I was of the opinion that injury No.1 caused by sharp edge weapon which damaged the major blood vessels and tissues of neck leading to massive hemorrhages and shock was sufficient to cause death in the normal course of life.” M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 6 On the same day, Dr. Sumera Sarwar (PW-15) also conducted the postmortem examination of the dead body of Fatima Bibi daughter of Zafar Hussain (deceased). Dr. Sumera Sarwar (PW-15), on examining the dead body of the deceased namely Fatima Bibi daughter of Zafar Hussain (deceased), observed as under:- “Final opinion. After examining the reports of Histopathologist, Forensic Toxicological Analysis, Forensic Serology analysis and Radiology, my final opinion regarding the cause of death is ante- mortem drowning. My final opinion Ex.P-K/4 is in my handwriting and signed by me.” Dr. Sumera Sarwar (PW-15) also stated that she had received the report of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PBB) regarding the analysis of the hyoid bone and other viscera of the deceased namely Fatima Bibi daughter of Zafar Hussain. The following relevant analysis was made in the said report (Exh.PBB):- “The adjacent soft tissue and muscle of neck reveals blood haemorrhages suggesting ante-mortem nature of injury at the level of neck.”(emphasis supplied) The prosecution also got Dr. Shaista Anwar (PW-16) examined who stated that she had been working with Dr.Rawish Nawaz ( the presence of Dr. Rawish Nawaz could not be procured) and was familiar with her handwriting. Dr. Shaista Anwar (PW-16) stated that Dr. Rawish Nawaz had examined Azra Bibi (PW-4) on 15.10.2013 and issued the Medico Legal Examination Certificate (Exh.PL) and had also examined Zakia Bibi (PW-3) on the same day and had issued the Medico Legal Examination Certificate (Exh.PM). The prosecution also got Dr. Ghazala Ahmadani (PW-5) examined who

The prosecution also got Dr. Ghazala Ahmadani (PW-5) examined who M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 7 stated that on 03.01.2014 she was posted as WMO at Sheikh Zayed Hospital Rahim Yar Khan and on the same day she examined Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and determined that there was no dental trauma suffered by Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and issued her report (Exh.PD). On the same day Dr. Ghazala Ahmadani (PW-5) also examined Azra Bibi (PW-4) and observed injuries on the teeth of Azra Bibi (PW-4) and issued her report (Exh.PE). The prosecution also got Dr. Anees ur Rehman (PW- 10) examined who stated that on 30.12.2013 he was posted as Assistant Professor ENT at Sheikh Zayed Hospital Rahim Yar Khan and on the same day examined Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and found her hearing to be normal and issued his report (Exh.PH). The prosecution also got Dr. Salman Ahmad (PW-17) examined who stated that on 28.12.2013 he was posted at Sheikh Zayed Hospital Rahim Yar Khan and on the same day issued his report (Exh.PV) with regard to the injuries observed on the person Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and his report (Exh.PW) with regard to the injuries observed on the person of Azra Bibi (PW-4) { the reports Exh.PB and Exh.PW show that Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and Azra Bibi (PW-4) had been admitted in the Sheikh Zayed Hospital Rahim Yar Khan since 14.10.2013}. The prosecution also got Dr. Asif Javed (PW-19) examined who stated that he had been working with Dr. Kashif Nadeem, ( the presence of Dr. Kashif Nadeem could not be procured) and on 14.10.2013 Dr. Kashif Nadeem had examined Azra Bibi (PW-4) who had suffered fracture of occipital bone and the report of Dr. Kashif Nadeem was (Exh.PY). 7. On 23.10.2017, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor gave up the prosecution witnesses namely Haji Naziq and

Kashif Nadeem was (Exh.PY). 7. On 23.10.2017, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor gave up the prosecution witnesses namely Haji Naziq and M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 8 Sabir Hussain as being unnecessary. On 21.02.2018, the learned ADPP closed the prosecution evidence after tendering in evidence the report of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PZ) regarding the blood stained earth and the external and internal vaginal swabs of the deceased, the report of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PAA) regarding the analysis of the viscera of the deceased and the report of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PBB) regarding the analysis of the hyoid bone and other viscera of the deceased namely Fatima Bibi daughter of Zafar Hussain. 8. After the closure of prosecution evidence, the learned trial court examined the appellant namely Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh under section 342 Cr.P.C. and in answer to question why this case against you and why the PWs have deposed against you, he replied that he had been involved in the case falsely and was innocent and the occurrence had been committed by his brother namely Zafar Hussain, father of Fatima Bibi (deceased). The appellant namely Muhammad Imran son of Hazoor Bakhsh opted not to get himself examined under section 340 Cr.P.C and did not adduce any evidence in his defence. 9. On the conclusion of the trial, the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Rahim Yar Khan, convicted and sentenced the appellant as referred to above. 10. The contention of the learned counsel for the appellant is that whole case is fabricated and false. The learned counsel for the appellant argued that the prosecution remained unable to prove the M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 9 facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible and relevant evidence to prove the same.

The learned counsel for the appellant argued that the prosecution remained unable to prove the M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 9 facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible and relevant evidence to prove the same. The learned counsel for the appellant further contended that the statements of Jam Haji (PW-1), Ghulam Raziq (PW-2), Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and Azra Bibi (PW-4) were not worthy of any reliance. The learned counsel for the appellant also argued that the recoveries were full of procedural defects, of no legal worth and value and result of fake proceedings. The learned counsel for the appellant finally submitted that the prosecution has totally failed to prove the case against the appellant beyond the shadow of doubt. 11. On the other hand, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant contended that the prosecution has proved its case beyond shadow of doubt by producing independent witnesses. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant further argued that both the deceased died as a result of injuries suffered at the hands of the appellant. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General alongwith the learned counsel for the complainant further contended that the medical evidence also corroborated the statements of Jam Haji (PW- 1), Ghulam Raziq (PW-2), Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and Azra Bibi (PW-4). The learned Deputy Prosecutor General alongwith the learned counsel for the complainant further argued that the recovery of the hatchet (P- 1) also corroborated the ocular account. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant further contended that there was no occasion for the prosecution witnesses, who were related to the deceased, to substitute the real M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 10 offender with the innocent in this case.

The learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant further contended that there was no occasion for the prosecution witnesses, who were related to the deceased, to substitute the real M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 10 offender with the innocent in this case. Lastly, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant prayed for the rejection of appeal. 12. We have heard the learned counsel for the appellant, the learned counsel for the complainant, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General and with their assistance have perused the record and evidence recorded during the trial. 13. The whole prosecution case revolves around the statements of Jam Haji (PW-1), Ghulam Raziq (PW-2), Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and Azra Bibi (PW-4). A perusal of the record reveals that the examination in chief of the Jam Haji (PW-1) was recorded on 24.01.2017, after the charge had been framed on 21.04.2014, however, prior to the cross-examination of Jam Haji (PW-1), the said witness died on 15.05.2017, hence he could not be cross examined. The submission of the learned counsel for the appellant is that unless Jam Haji (PW-1) was subjected to cross-examination,such piece of evidence had no evidentiary value, therefore, it was not be taken into consideration or made basis for awarding conviction to the appellant. The learned counsel for the appellant, however, could not cite any specific provision of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 or any other law in force in support of such submission. The moot point for consideration before us is as to what could be legal value of the evidence of Jam Haji (PW-1) in the form of his examination-in-chief, when he had died before he could be subjected to cross-examination by the appellant. By virtue of Article 47 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, evidence given by a witness in a judicial proceeding or before M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl.

The moot point for consideration before us is as to what could be legal value of the evidence of Jam Haji (PW-1) in the form of his examination-in-chief, when he had died before he could be subjected to cross-examination by the appellant. By virtue of Article 47 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, evidence given by a witness in a judicial proceeding or before M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 11 any person authorized by law to take it, is relevant for the purpose of proving the same in a subsequent judicial proceeding or at a later stage of the same judicial proceeding, the truth of the facts which it states, when the witness is dead or cannot be found or is incapable of giving evidence. Article 47 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 provides as under:- “ 47. Relevancy of certain evidence for proving, in subsequent proceeding, the truth of facts therein stated.---Evidence given by a witness in a judicial proceeding, or before any person authorized by law to take it, is relevant for the purpose of proving, in a subsequent judicial proceeding, or in a later stage of the same judicial proceedings, the truth of the facts which it states, when the witness is dead or cannot be found, or is incapable of giving evidence, or is kept out of the way by the adverse party, or if his presence cannot be obtained without an amount of delay or expense which, under the circumstances of the case, the Court considers unreasonable:--- Provided that:--- the proceeding was between the same parties or their representatives-in-interest; the adverse party in the first proceedings had the right and opportunity to cross-examine; the questions in issue were substantially the same in the first as in the second proceeding. Explanation.---A criminal trial or inquiry shall be deemed to be a proceeding between the prosecutor and the accused within the meaning of the Article.” Article 47 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 visualizes relevancy and significance to the evidence of a witness in a judicial proceeding or before any person authorized by law to take evidence, when the said witness is dead or cannot be found or is incapable of giving evidence, subject to the conditions, provided in the proviso to the said Article, that the proceedings were between the same parties or their representative-in-interest, which for the purpose of criminal trial or inquiry shall be deemed to be a proceeding between the prosecutor and the accused within the meaning of the said Article; when the

Relevancy of certain evidence for proving, in subsequent proceeding, the truth of facts therein stated.---Evidence given by a witness in a judicial proceeding, or before any person authorized by law to take it, is relevant for the purpose of proving, in a subsequent judicial proceeding, or in a later stage of the same judicial proceedings, the truth of the facts which it states, when the witness is dead or cannot be found, or is incapable of giving evidence, or is kept out of the way by the adverse party, or if his presence cannot be obtained without an amount of delay or expense which, under the circumstances of the case, the Court considers unreasonable:--- Provided that:--- the proceeding was between the same parties or their representatives-in-interest; the adverse party in the first proceedings had the right and opportunity to cross-examine; the questions in issue were substantially the same in the first as in the second proceeding. Explanation.---A criminal trial or inquiry shall be deemed to be a proceeding between the prosecutor and the accused within the meaning of the Article.” Article 47 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 visualizes relevancy and significance to the evidence of a witness in a judicial proceeding or before any person authorized by law to take evidence, when the said witness is dead or cannot be found or is incapable of giving evidence, subject to the conditions, provided in the proviso to the said Article, that the proceedings were between the same parties or their representative-in-interest, which for the purpose of criminal trial or inquiry shall be deemed to be a proceeding between the prosecutor and the accused within the meaning of the said Article; when the M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 12 adverse party in the first proceedings had the right and opportunity to cross-examine; the questions in issue were substantially the same in the first as in the second proceeding. Article 131 of the Qanun-e- Shahadat, 1984 leaves at the discretion of the Judge to decide admissibility of any evidence and for this purpose gives wide powers to him subject to the language of this Article. More so, as there is no provision in the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order which specifically makes such piece of evidence inadmissible. A perusal of the record reveals that the examination in chief of the Jam Haji (PW-1) was recorded on 24.01.2017, after the charge had been framed on 21.04.2014, however, prior to the cross-examination of Jam Haji (PW-1), the said witness died on 15.05.2017, hence he could not be cross examined by the appellant.The order sheet of the learned trial court reflects that Jam Haji (PW-1) appeared before the same on numerous occasions but his evidence was not recorded due to the failure of the appellant and the non-availability of his counsel which ended with the result that Jam Haji (PW-1) died and could not be cross examined by the appellant. It is not a case that the statement of Jam Haji (PW-1) was not recorded due to the absence of the appellant rather in this case the appellant simply did not cross examine Jam Haji (PW-1) till he remained alive, though Jam Haji (PW-1) kept appearing before the learned trial court for the said purpose. The appellant in a deliberate and calculated manner himself avoided to avail such an opportunity. Therefore we are holding that the evidence of Jam Haji (PW-1), in the form of examination-in-chief, was an admissible piece of evidence, which could be legally taken into consideration by the Court in the

The appellant in a deliberate and calculated manner himself avoided to avail such an opportunity. Therefore we are holding that the evidence of Jam Haji (PW-1), in the form of examination-in-chief, was an admissible piece of evidence, which could be legally taken into consideration by the Court in the M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 13 peculiar facts and circumstances. Reliance in this regard is placed on the case of ARBAB TASLEEM VS. THE STATE (P L D 2010 Supreme Court 642) wherein the august Supreme Court of Pakistan has held as under:- “16. After careful analysis of the legal and factual position, involved in this case, we cannot resist, but to say that the evidence of P.W.11, in the form of examination-in-chief, was an admissible piece of evidence, which could be legally taken into consideration by the Court in the peculiar facts and circumstances, but with extra care and caution, which has already been taken by the two Courts below, as in this case such evidence of P. W.11 is fully corroborated from the medical evidence and other surrounding circumstances of the case. 17. To put it bluntly, the manner in which the proceedings before the Sessions Court were delayed, due to the conduct, acts of omissions of the appellant or otherwise, the Trial Court cannot be absolved of its lethargy, inaction or to some extent, negligence, which ended in the incident dated 20-1-2004 of the murder of P.W. 11 on the date of hearing i.e. 20-1-2004. 18. Indeed, legally and technically examination-in-chief of P.W.11, recorded on 18-12-2003, may not be termed as a statement under section 512, Cr.P.C. in the strict sense, nor Appellate Court in its impugned judgment had held so, but the fact remains that in the peculiar facts and circumstances of the case, the manner in which proceedings in the Sessions case were delayed; the star eye-witnesses of the incident were repeatedly required to appear in Court for their evidence, the circumstances under which examination-in-chief of P.W.11 was recorded before the Trial Court on 18-12-2003; the conduct of the appellant and his counsel in Court on 18-12-2003; non-availing of the opportunity of cross-examination even thereafter for a period of over one month and three dates of hearing, are the material facts which could not be to give benefit of such delay in the proceedings of Sessions Case to the appellant.

To put it bluntly, the manner in which the proceedings before the Sessions Court were delayed, due to the conduct, acts of omissions of the appellant or otherwise, the Trial Court cannot be absolved of its lethargy, inaction or to some extent, negligence, which ended in the incident dated 20-1-2004 of the murder of P.W. 11 on the date of hearing i.e. 20-1-2004. 18. Indeed, legally and technically examination-in-chief of P.W.11, recorded on 18-12-2003, may not be termed as a statement under section 512, Cr.P.C. in the strict sense, nor Appellate Court in its impugned judgment had held so, but the fact remains that in the peculiar facts and circumstances of the case, the manner in which proceedings in the Sessions case were delayed; the star eye-witnesses of the incident were repeatedly required to appear in Court for their evidence, the circumstances under which examination-in-chief of P.W.11 was recorded before the Trial Court on 18-12-2003; the conduct of the appellant and his counsel in Court on 18-12-2003; non-availing of the opportunity of cross-examination even thereafter for a period of over one month and three dates of hearing, are the material facts which could not be to give benefit of such delay in the proceedings of Sessions Case to the appellant. There is hardly any need to observe that law is a living organ and it is the duty of the Court to adopt a realistic and pragmatic approach for its application, looking to the peculiar facts and circumstances of each case. In the instant case, as discussed above in detailed, it cannot be said that the evidence of P.W. 11 in the form of his examination-in-chief is liable to be rejected to simple proposition that he was not subject to cross- examination, but it is case where in a deliberate and calculated Manner the appellant himself avoided to avail such an opportunity. To put it differently, a distinction drawn between the two situations, one where opportunity to cross-examination was not given or denied to a party, and second where it was given but deliberately or otherwise not availed of. Instant case, undoubtedly, falls under the second category, where ample opportunity was given but not availed by the appellant we have, therefore, no reason to disbelieve or disagree with the observation of the trial Court that appellant's side deliberately avoided to cross-examine deceased P.W. Jehanzeb. Even the trial Court, to a greater extent, cannot be blamed in not engaging a State counsel on behalf of the appellant in the matters, as on one hand at one stage of proceedings such request was made by the appellant to the Court, which was kept on hollow hopes nor the Court was given such impression to enable it to undertake such exercise to avoid further delay in the cross-examination of P.W.11. Seemingly, the trial Court exercised restraint in appointing State counsel M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 14 on behalf of the appellant as it might have given the appellant yet another cause of grievance to challenge it before the higher forum, and in this manner to further delay the conclusion of the trial. 19.

Seemingly, the trial Court exercised restraint in appointing State counsel M.R No. 07 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 176-J of 2018 14 on behalf of the appellant as it might have given the appellant yet another cause of grievance to challenge it before the higher forum, and in this manner to further delay the conclusion of the trial. 19. Learned counsel for the appellant appearing before us could not controvert at all that upon taking into consideration the evidence of P.W.11, the only eye-witness of the incident examined by the trial Court, the prosecution succeeded to prove its case beyond reasonable doubt against the appellant and, thus, the concurrent findings of the two forums below against the appellant regarding his conviction and sentence are unexceptionable.” 14. As mentioned above, the whole prosecution case revolves around the statements of Jam Haji (PW-1), Ghulam Raziq (PW-2), Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and Azra Bibi (PW-4). These witnesses namely Jam Haji (PW-1), Ghulam Raziq (PW-2), Zakia Bibi (PW-3) and Azra Bibi (PW-4) were related to each other and the deceased and the appellant also. Article 21 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 provides that the fact that any person accused of a crime absconded after the same is a relevant fact to prove the fact in issue. Illustration (i) of Article 21 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 reads as under:- “(i) A is accused of a crime. The facts that, after the commission of the alleged crime, he absconded, or was in possession of property or the proceeds of property acquired by the crime, or attempted to conceal things which were or might have been used in committing it, are relevant.” This conduct of the appellant to evade the law after the occurrence is a further indicator pointing towards of his guilt when considered in conjunction with the ocular evidence in the case.

It is well recognized principle by now that the question of quantum of sentence requires utmost attention and thoughtfulness on the parts of the Courts. In this regard we respectfully refer the case of Mir Muhammad alias Miro v. The State (2009 SCMR 1188) wherein august Supreme Court has held as under:- "It will not be out of place to emphasize that in criminal cases, the question of quantum of sentence requires utmost care and caution on the parts of the Courts, as such decisions restrict the life and liberties of the people. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan has held in the case of “Ansar Ahmad Khan Barki v. The State and another” (1993 SCMR 1660), that the prosecution is bound by law to exclude all possible extenuating circumstances in order to bring the charge home to the accused for the award of normal penalty of death.

The difference of punishment for Qatl-i-amd as Qisas and Tazir provided under sections 302(a) and 302(b), P.P.C. respectively is that in a case of Qisas, Court has no discretion in the matter of sentence whereas in case of Tazir Court may award either of the sentence provided under section 302(b), P.P.C. and exercise of this direction in the case of sentence of Tazir would depend upon the facts and circumstances of the case.
